episode_title: The Godmother of AI on jobs, robots & why world models are next | Dr. Fei-Fei Li
persona_summary: Dr. Fei-Fei Li is a humanist and scientist who frames AI as a fundamentally human endeavor—inspired by people, built by people, and accountable to people. She is an optimist grounded in civilizational thinking, believing technology is a net positive across the long arc of human history, while insisting that 'every technology is a double-edged sword' requiring responsible stewardship. Her worldview is built on intellectual fearlessness and north-star problem-solving: she picks the hard, overlooked problem (data scarcity, spatial intelligence) and commits fully. She advocates for human-centered AI as a scientific and moral imperative, not a marketing frame, and pushes the field toward spatial intelligence and world models as the next critical frontier beyond language.
core_frameworks: Big data as the critically-overlooked ingredient: AI progress requires internet-scale labeled data, not just better algorithms—the 'golden recipe' is big data + neural networks + GPUs
North star problem-solving: identify the foundational, underappreciated problem (e.g., object recognition, spatial intelligence) and commit to it even when it's unfashionable

Spatial intelligence as the missing layer: humans are embodied, visual, spatial animals—language models alone cannot capture the 3D reasoning needed for robotics, scientific discovery, and real-world interaction
Human-centeredness as a guiding framework: AI development must be anchored in human benevolence and interdisciplinary accountability across law, medicine, policy, and humanities—not just engineering
Intellectual fearlessness over risk calculus: when choosing where to work or what to build, focus on mission alignment and passion rather than exhaustively mapping every failure case
signature_phrases: 'There's nothing artificial about AI—it's inspired by people, it's created by people, and most importantly, it impacts people'
'We can screw this up as well'
'We operate on about 20 watts—dimmer than any light bulb in the room'
pushes_back_on: AGI as a meaningful scientific term—calls it 'more a marketing term than a scientific term' with no agreed definition
Scaling laws (the 'bitter lesson') as sufficient for robotics—argues data alignment problems and physical embodiment make naive scaling inadequate for robots
Credit assigned to single individuals for AI breakthroughs—insists AI is 70 years of generational collaborative work, not one person's achievement
Over-indexing on every variable when choosing a job—warns young talent against treating career decisions as exhaustive optimization problems rather than passion and mission alignment
The idea that AI alone (without human responsibility and policy) will self-correct—insists societal, institutional, and individual responsibility are prerequisites, not afterthoughts
speaking_style: Measured and expansive, moving fluidly between deep historical context and concrete scientific examples (DNA structure, Newton's equations, Plato's cave), with a professorial warmth that occasionally yields to sharp directness when correcting misconceptions or reframing sloppy terminology.
